item.title: SAMDFuse: Semantic-Aware Multi-scale Degradation-robust Fusion network for SAR and optical images
item.dc:creator: Ruoxi Wang
Yinhe Li
Enhua Zhang
Wanni Chen
Kaizhi Wang
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Robust fusion of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and optical imagery is critical for improving the performance of downstream tasks, such as land classification and target monitoring. However, existing fusion frameworks exhibit limited capability to suppress the inherent speckle noise in SAR images and lack effective mechanisms for handling degradation factors in optical images, such as cloud occlusion. Additionally, most current fusion algorithms prioritize visual enhancement, providing limited benefits for downstream tasks. To address these limitations, we propose a degradation-robust and semantic-aware SAR–optical image fusion network with three main contributions. First, an encoder architecture is designed with Sparse Feature Extraction Module (SFEM) layers. SFEM integrates Sparse Self-Attention (SSA) with a Hybrid-Scale Feed-forward Network (HSFN) to suppress speckle noise and enhance stable cross-modal discriminative features. Second, a degradation-aware data augmentation strategy is developed by simulating realistic cloud occlusions, encouraging the network to learn complementary cross-modal information and improving its generalization under complex conditions. Third, Semantic-Guided Fusion Modules (SGFMs) inject multi-scale semantic features into the decoder, enabling the fused results to maintain spectral and structural consistency while better supporting downstream semantic segmentation. Experiments on the WHU-OPT-SAR dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches such as FusionMamba and VSFF, achieving a structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.968, a visual information fidelity fusion (VIFF) of 0.498, and a mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 52.82%. Consistent improvements are also observed on additional datasets, while robust structural preservation and semantic consistency are maintained under severe degradations. The project is available at: https://github.com/christie6133/SAMDFuse . Published: 2026-05-09T13:07:28+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.846 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruoxi Wang; Yinhe Li; Enhua Zhang; Wanni Chen; Kaizhi Wang

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.049

Relevance: 0.846 (must_read)

Robust fusion of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and optical imagery is critical for improving the performance of downstream tasks, such as land classification and target monitoring. However, existing fusion frameworks exhibit limited capability to suppress the inherent speckle noise in SAR images and lack effective mechanisms for handling degradation factors in optical images, such as cloud occlusion. Additionally, most current fusion algorithms prioritize visual enhancement, providing limited benefits for downstream tasks. To address these limitations, we propose a degradation-robust and semantic-aware SAR–optical image fusion network with three main contributions. First, an encoder architecture is designed with Sparse Feature Extraction Module (SFEM) layers. SFEM integrates Sparse Self-Attention (SSA) with a Hybrid-Scale Feed-forward Network (HSFN) to suppress speckle noise and enhance stable cross-modal discriminative features. Second, a degradation-aware data augmentation strategy is developed by simulating realistic cloud occlusions, encouraging the network to learn complementary cross-modal information and improving its generalization under complex conditions. Third, Semantic-Guided Fusion Modules (SGFMs) inject multi-scale semantic features into the decoder, enabling the fused results to maintain spectral and structural consistency while better supporting downstream semantic segmentation. Experiments on the WHU-OPT-SAR dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches such as FusionMamba and VSFF, achieving a structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.968, a visual information fidelity fusion (VIFF) of 0.498, and a mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 52.82%. Consistent improvements are also observed on additional datasets, while robust structural preservation and semantic consistency are maintained under severe degradations. The project is available at: https://github.com/christie6133/SAMDFuse .

item.title: Na-IRSTD: Enhancing Infrared Small Target Detection via Native-Resolution Feature Selection and Fusion
item.dc:creator: Qian Xu
Chi Zhang
Qiming Zhang
Xi Li
Haojuan Yuan
Mingjin Zhang
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks. Published: 2026-05-07T07:42:03+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.814 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qian Xu; Chi Zhang; Qiming Zhang; Xi Li; Haojuan Yuan; Mingjin Zhang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.814 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

item.title: StratiFormer: Stratified Temporal Transformer for video object detection
item.dc:creator: Wentao Zheng
Hong Zheng
Yuquan Sun
Ying Jing
item.description: In video object detection (VOD), exploiting temporal cues is essential for alleviating common visual degradations (e.g., motion blur and occlusion). Existing approaches range from short-range feature enhancement to long-range dependency aggregation, while some attempt to combine both. Although substantial progress has been achieved, most designs still decouple temporal aggregation from multi-scale feature construction. In particular, many methods adopt a unified temporal aggregation range across all feature levels, which is level-agnostic and overlooks the distinct temporal requirements of low-level features for fine-grained localization and high-level features for abstract semantics. This limitation restricts a model’s ability to adaptively utilize temporal information according to the feature hierarchy. To address this issue, we propose the Stratified Temporal Transformer (StratiFormer). StratiFormer integrates the fusion of long- and short-range temporal cues directly into the cross-scale feature construction process, enabling feature-level-adaptive temporal aggregation. Moreover, it employs a stratified selection mechanism to identify and process a sparse yet informative set of temporal features at each feature level, allowing the model to allocate computation to the most relevant candidates. On the ImageNet VID benchmark, StratiFormer achieves up to 88.2% mAP 50 " role="presentation"> mAP 50 mAP 50 , delivering a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency. Published: 2026-05-09T15:15:25+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wentao Zheng; Hong Zheng; Yuquan Sun; Ying Jing

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116117

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

In video object detection (VOD), exploiting temporal cues is essential for alleviating common visual degradations (e.g., motion blur and occlusion). Existing approaches range from short-range feature enhancement to long-range dependency aggregation, while some attempt to combine both. Although substantial progress has been achieved, most designs still decouple temporal aggregation from multi-scale feature construction. In particular, many methods adopt a unified temporal aggregation range across all feature levels, which is level-agnostic and overlooks the distinct temporal requirements of low-level features for fine-grained localization and high-level features for abstract semantics. This limitation restricts a model’s ability to adaptively utilize temporal information according to the feature hierarchy. To address this issue, we propose the Stratified Temporal Transformer (StratiFormer). StratiFormer integrates the fusion of long- and short-range temporal cues directly into the cross-scale feature construction process, enabling feature-level-adaptive temporal aggregation. Moreover, it employs a stratified selection mechanism to identify and process a sparse yet informative set of temporal features at each feature level, allowing the model to allocate computation to the most relevant candidates. On the ImageNet VID benchmark, StratiFormer achieves up to 88.2% mAP 50 " role="presentation"> mAP 50 mAP 50 , delivering a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency.

item.title: Efficient spectral-temporal reconstruction of long-term satellite time series via temporal segments and mask-informed embedding
item.dc:creator: Jie Yang
Xin Huang
item.description: Long-term satellite time series (STS) provide invaluable Earth observations for monitoring global change. However, they suffer from a high proportion of missing values due to cloud cover, shadows, and sensor limitations. Reconstructing these incomplete datasets to create a continuous spectral-temporal record is therefore crucial for maximizing their scientific value. While existing Transformer-based architectures show promise, they still face challenges in maintaining spectral consistency and ensuring robustness against complex missingness. In this paper, we propose mask-informed spectral-temporal Transformer (MISTR), a novel framework that addresses these challenges by first partitioning the original multi-spectral sequence into a set of parallel, single-band temporal segments. Building on this, we propose the mask-informed embedding (MIE) module, which generates feature embeddings that are robust to the underlying missingness, thereby explicitly enhancing the ability to represent incomplete data. The resulting stable embeddings are then processed by the temporal-spectral adaptive (TSA) blocks, which model temporal dynamics across time and spectral dependencies across bands, while implementing adaptive information flow control through a gate mechanism. We conducted experiments on 30 Landsat ARD tiles over China, spanning from 1997 to 2023. The results show that MISTR achieves competitive reconstruction accuracy, with a modest but consistent improvement over the strongest baseline when applied to multi-year satellite time series, while yielding better computational efficiency. Beyond pointwise reconstruction, MISTR learns more stable spectral–temporal representations that transfer effectively to downstream tasks, including land cover classification, change detection, and vegetation phenology. In addition, MISTR demonstrates cross-sensor flexibility for adaptation to Sentinel-2 and MODIS, and we further present a preliminary joint-sensor setting for collaborative reconstruction under sparse conditions. In summary, these results highlight the potential of MISTR for applications in the field of long-term STS analysis. Published: 2026-05-09T15:50:08+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jie Yang; Xin Huang

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115470

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Long-term satellite time series (STS) provide invaluable Earth observations for monitoring global change. However, they suffer from a high proportion of missing values due to cloud cover, shadows, and sensor limitations. Reconstructing these incomplete datasets to create a continuous spectral-temporal record is therefore crucial for maximizing their scientific value. While existing Transformer-based architectures show promise, they still face challenges in maintaining spectral consistency and ensuring robustness against complex missingness. In this paper, we propose mask-informed spectral-temporal Transformer (MISTR), a novel framework that addresses these challenges by first partitioning the original multi-spectral sequence into a set of parallel, single-band temporal segments. Building on this, we propose the mask-informed embedding (MIE) module, which generates feature embeddings that are robust to the underlying missingness, thereby explicitly enhancing the ability to represent incomplete data. The resulting stable embeddings are then processed by the temporal-spectral adaptive (TSA) blocks, which model temporal dynamics across time and spectral dependencies across bands, while implementing adaptive information flow control through a gate mechanism. We conducted experiments on 30 Landsat ARD tiles over China, spanning from 1997 to 2023. The results show that MISTR achieves competitive reconstruction accuracy, with a modest but consistent improvement over the strongest baseline when applied to multi-year satellite time series, while yielding better computational efficiency. Beyond pointwise reconstruction, MISTR learns more stable spectral–temporal representations that transfer effectively to downstream tasks, including land cover classification, change detection, and vegetation phenology. In addition, MISTR demonstrates cross-sensor flexibility for adaptation to Sentinel-2 and MODIS, and we further present a preliminary joint-sensor setting for collaborative reconstruction under sparse conditions. In summary, these results highlight the potential of MISTR for applications in the field of long-term STS analysis.

item.title: HYOLO: A Lightweight and Efficient Detection Framework for Small Objects in Aerial Images
item.dc:creator: Shixiao Wu
Huafeng Kong
Zhiying Xu
item.description: Detecting small objects in UAV and remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge due to extremely limited pixel coverage, sparse feature representation, frequent occlusion, and complex backgrounds. Existing methods suffer from three common shortcomings in small-object detection: (1) conventional feature extraction paradigms struggle to capture discriminative features from cluttered backgrounds, leading to the loss of critical small-object features; (2) multi-scale fusion networks in existing lightweight models exhibit insufficient cross-level interaction and representation capacity; and (3) direct detection after fusion often neglects spatially critical regions, substantially degrading small-object accuracy. To address these issues, this paper proposes HYOLO, a novel framework for efficient and accurate small-object detection in complex aerial scenes. The framework introduces three core innovations: (1) a heterogeneous Multi-branch Dynamic Feature Fusion Module (MBDF) that combines complementary feature extraction pathways (micro-object, depthwise separable, and directionally asymmetric convolutions) via dual-attention fusion and cross-stage reuse; (2) a Recursive Attention Feature Pyramid Network (RAFPN) that establishes a closed-loop recursive architecture through three cascaded PANet modules with integrated attention refinement, enabling iterative enhancement of multi-scale feature representations while preserving critical spatial details; and (3) a Multi-scale Attentive Refinement Module for Small Objects (MARS) that employs stabilized dual-attention, Gaussian-based detail extraction, and parameter-controlled fusion. Evaluated on four challenging aerial benchmarks (VisDrone 2019, DIOR, UCAS-AOD, USOD), HYOLO achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy (e.g., 51.1% mAP50 on VisDrone, 97.9% on UCAS-AOD, 92.2% on USOD) while requiring only 9.3M parameters and 103.8 GFLOPs. Notably, HYOLO attains superior performance with 2–5 × fewer parameters and 1.5–3 × lower computational costs than comparable state-of-the-art models. This efficiency–performance trade-off positions HYOLO as a practical solution for resource-constrained aerial applications. Published: 2026-05-09T23:10:58+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shixiao Wu; Huafeng Kong; Zhiying Xu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132794

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Detecting small objects in UAV and remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge due to extremely limited pixel coverage, sparse feature representation, frequent occlusion, and complex backgrounds. Existing methods suffer from three common shortcomings in small-object detection: (1) conventional feature extraction paradigms struggle to capture discriminative features from cluttered backgrounds, leading to the loss of critical small-object features; (2) multi-scale fusion networks in existing lightweight models exhibit insufficient cross-level interaction and representation capacity; and (3) direct detection after fusion often neglects spatially critical regions, substantially degrading small-object accuracy. To address these issues, this paper proposes HYOLO, a novel framework for efficient and accurate small-object detection in complex aerial scenes. The framework introduces three core innovations: (1) a heterogeneous Multi-branch Dynamic Feature Fusion Module (MBDF) that combines complementary feature extraction pathways (micro-object, depthwise separable, and directionally asymmetric convolutions) via dual-attention fusion and cross-stage reuse; (2) a Recursive Attention Feature Pyramid Network (RAFPN) that establishes a closed-loop recursive architecture through three cascaded PANet modules with integrated attention refinement, enabling iterative enhancement of multi-scale feature representations while preserving critical spatial details; and (3) a Multi-scale Attentive Refinement Module for Small Objects (MARS) that employs stabilized dual-attention, Gaussian-based detail extraction, and parameter-controlled fusion. Evaluated on four challenging aerial benchmarks (VisDrone 2019, DIOR, UCAS-AOD, USOD), HYOLO achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy (e.g., 51.1% mAP50 on VisDrone, 97.9% on UCAS-AOD, 92.2% on USOD) while requiring only 9.3M parameters and 103.8 GFLOPs. Notably, HYOLO attains superior performance with 2–5 × fewer parameters and 1.5–3 × lower computational costs than comparable state-of-the-art models. This efficiency–performance trade-off positions HYOLO as a practical solution for resource-constrained aerial applications.

item.title: Learning Image-Adaptive Scale Fields for Metric Depth Recovery
item.dc:creator: Yuanyan Li
Matthias Althoff
item.description: Monocular depth estimation (MDE) typically produces depth estimations that are defined up to an unknown scale or shift. When only sparse metric anchors are available, recovering accurate metric depth becomes challenging yet necessary for practical applications. We address this problem by formulating metric depth recovery as image-adaptive scale field modeling. Instead of directly correcting the depth, we reformulate the correction as a low-dimensional linear combination of image-adaptive basis maps. These maps are derived from semantic and geometric cues encoded in the MDE estimations and intermediate representations. The weights of basis maps are efficiently determined from sparse metric anchors via a least-squares problem. This formulation yields improved metric depth accuracy, strong robustness under extreme anchor sparsity, and an interpretable decomposition of spatial scale variations. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and representative MDE models demonstrate the effectiveness and general applicability of our approach. Published: 2026-05-08T08:13:02+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuanyan Li; Matthias Althoff

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Monocular depth estimation (MDE) typically produces depth estimations that are defined up to an unknown scale or shift. When only sparse metric anchors are available, recovering accurate metric depth becomes challenging yet necessary for practical applications. We address this problem by formulating metric depth recovery as image-adaptive scale field modeling. Instead of directly correcting the depth, we reformulate the correction as a low-dimensional linear combination of image-adaptive basis maps. These maps are derived from semantic and geometric cues encoded in the MDE estimations and intermediate representations. The weights of basis maps are efficiently determined from sparse metric anchors via a least-squares problem. This formulation yields improved metric depth accuracy, strong robustness under extreme anchor sparsity, and an interpretable decomposition of spatial scale variations. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and representative MDE models demonstrate the effectiveness and general applicability of our approach.

item.title: TGRF-CLIP: CLIP-Based Text-Guided Fusion of Visual Residuals for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
item.dc:creator: Hongliang Yan
Xinshun Xu
item.description: Few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) remains a critical challenge in industrial quality inspection. Existing methods based on Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) typically freeze the text encoder to preserve generalization, relying solely on hand-crafted prompt templates and end-to-end training, which limits their ability to dynamically adapt text features to industrial defects. To address these limitations, we propose CLIP-Based Text-Guided Residual Fusion(TGRF-CLIP), a text-guided fusion of visual residuals framework that enhances CLIP’s defect recognition capability while maintaining its generalization. Specifically, TGRF-CLIP is implemented through a two-stage training strategy that progresses from dedicated optimization to joint optimization. First, a lightweight text adapter and semantic projection layer learn industrial normal/anomaly semantics, reducing the reliance on hand-crafted prompt engineering during inference. Second, multi-level visual residuals capture local and global features that are fused under text semantics guidance in joint training, achieving defect-oriented fusion and preventing a disconnect between general and defect-specific features. Extensive experiments show that TGRF-CLIP outperforms existing methods under few-shot settings, achieving competitive AUROC scores of 90.7% on VisA and 97.0% on MVTec AD. Published: 2026-05-09T06:52:16+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hongliang Yan; Xinshun Xu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132817

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

Few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) remains a critical challenge in industrial quality inspection. Existing methods based on Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) typically freeze the text encoder to preserve generalization, relying solely on hand-crafted prompt templates and end-to-end training, which limits their ability to dynamically adapt text features to industrial defects. To address these limitations, we propose CLIP-Based Text-Guided Residual Fusion(TGRF-CLIP), a text-guided fusion of visual residuals framework that enhances CLIP’s defect recognition capability while maintaining its generalization. Specifically, TGRF-CLIP is implemented through a two-stage training strategy that progresses from dedicated optimization to joint optimization. First, a lightweight text adapter and semantic projection layer learn industrial normal/anomaly semantics, reducing the reliance on hand-crafted prompt engineering during inference. Second, multi-level visual residuals capture local and global features that are fused under text semantics guidance in joint training, achieving defect-oriented fusion and preventing a disconnect between general and defect-specific features. Extensive experiments show that TGRF-CLIP outperforms existing methods under few-shot settings, achieving competitive AUROC scores of 90.7% on VisA and 97.0% on MVTec AD.

item.title: LoHGNet: Infrared Small Target Detection through Lorentz Geometric Encoding with High-Order Relation Learning
item.dc:creator: Qianwen Ma
Yang Xu
Shangwei Deng
Xiaobo Li
Haofeng Hu
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to the scarcity of useful target cues and the presence of severe background clutter. Most current methods rely on conventional feature learning and local interaction modeling, where features are represented in Euclidean space. However, such designs may still be limited in describing the subtle differences of weak targets and the contextual relations between targets and backgrounds. To address these limitations, we propose LoHGNet, an IRSTD network that integrates Lorentz geometric encoding with high-order relation learning. By introducing Lorentz manifold based feature learning, LoHGNet offers a different feature representation from conventional IRSTD methods and provides new discriminative cues for IRSTD. Specifically, a Lorentz encoding branch is constructed with the Geometric Attention Guided Lorentz Residual Convolution Module (GA-LRCM) to perform feature modeling under hyperbolic geometric constraints and enhance the hierarchical geometric representation capability of weak targets. Subsequently, the hyperbolic features are mapped into the Euclidean tangent space through logarithmic mapping, and a High-Order Relation Learning Module (HORL) is designed to model the high-order contextual dependencies between targets and backgrounds via hypergraph construction, thereby improving target discrimination in complex backgrounds. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed LoHGNet achieves competitive performance in both detection accuracy and adaptability to complex scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Kingwin97. Published: 2026-05-08T04:00:34+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qianwen Ma; Yang Xu; Shangwei Deng; Xiaobo Li; Haofeng Hu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to the scarcity of useful target cues and the presence of severe background clutter. Most current methods rely on conventional feature learning and local interaction modeling, where features are represented in Euclidean space. However, such designs may still be limited in describing the subtle differences of weak targets and the contextual relations between targets and backgrounds. To address these limitations, we propose LoHGNet, an IRSTD network that integrates Lorentz geometric encoding with high-order relation learning. By introducing Lorentz manifold based feature learning, LoHGNet offers a different feature representation from conventional IRSTD methods and provides new discriminative cues for IRSTD. Specifically, a Lorentz encoding branch is constructed with the Geometric Attention Guided Lorentz Residual Convolution Module (GA-LRCM) to perform feature modeling under hyperbolic geometric constraints and enhance the hierarchical geometric representation capability of weak targets. Subsequently, the hyperbolic features are mapped into the Euclidean tangent space through logarithmic mapping, and a High-Order Relation Learning Module (HORL) is designed to model the high-order contextual dependencies between targets and backgrounds via hypergraph construction, thereby improving target discrimination in complex backgrounds. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed LoHGNet achieves competitive performance in both detection accuracy and adaptability to complex scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Kingwin97.

item.title: A Marine Debris Detection Framework for Ocean Robots via Self-Attention Enhancement and Feature Interaction Optimization
item.dc:creator: Yuyang Li
Jiashu Han
Yinyi Lai
Wenbin Kang
Zenghui Liu
item.description: Marine debris detection for ocean robot is crucial for ecological protection, yet performance is often degraded by low-quality images with blur, complex backgrounds, and small targets. To address these challenges, we propose YOLO-MD, an enhanced YOLO-based detection framework. A Dual-Branch Convolutional Enhanced Self-Attention (DB-CASA) module is designed to strengthen spatial-channel interactions, improving feature representation in degraded images. Additionally, a lightweight shift-based operation is introduced to enhance fine-grained feature extraction for objects of varying scales while maintaining parameter efficiency. We further propose SFG-Loss to mitigate class imbalance and optimization instability via dynamic sample reweighting. Experiments on the UODM dataset demonstrate that YOLO-MD achieves 0.875 precision, 0.822 F1-score, and 0.849 mAP50, outperforming the latest state-of-the-art methods. The effectiveness of this method has also been verified through real-world robotic edge deployment experiments. Published: 2026-05-08T07:43:01+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuyang Li; Jiashu Han; Yinyi Lai; Wenbin Kang; Zenghui Liu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Marine debris detection for ocean robot is crucial for ecological protection, yet performance is often degraded by low-quality images with blur, complex backgrounds, and small targets. To address these challenges, we propose YOLO-MD, an enhanced YOLO-based detection framework. A Dual-Branch Convolutional Enhanced Self-Attention (DB-CASA) module is designed to strengthen spatial-channel interactions, improving feature representation in degraded images. Additionally, a lightweight shift-based operation is introduced to enhance fine-grained feature extraction for objects of varying scales while maintaining parameter efficiency. We further propose SFG-Loss to mitigate class imbalance and optimization instability via dynamic sample reweighting. Experiments on the UODM dataset demonstrate that YOLO-MD achieves 0.875 precision, 0.822 F1-score, and 0.849 mAP50, outperforming the latest state-of-the-art methods. The effectiveness of this method has also been verified through real-world robotic edge deployment experiments.

item.title: Beyond GSD-as-Token: Continuous Scale Conditioning for Remote Sensing VLMs
item.dc:creator: Song Zhang
Yanlong Chen
Yilin Li
Yining Chen
Zili Yi
Xiaowei Zhang
Yawei Li
item.description: Remote sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs) face a fundamental mismatch with natural-image counterparts: the same geographic object exhibits radically different visual evidence across ground sampling distances (GSDs) spanning multiple orders of magnitude. Yet existing RS-VLMs often discard GSD or inject it as a discrete text token, forcing a single static parameter set to absorb the entire scale spectrum. We introduce ScaleEarth, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework built on Qwen3-VL that treats GSD as a continuous conditioning variable governing the model's computation path. At its core, CS-HLoRA (Continuous Scale-Conditioned Hyper-LoRA) modulates the LoRA low-rank subspace through a GSD-driven gate, enabling the model to dynamically route computation by physical scale. To remove reliance on sensor metadata at deployment, we pair CS-HLoRA with SSE-U, a lightweight heteroscedastic sub-head that predicts GSD and its uncertainty from visual features. To provide matching supervision, we construct GeoScale-VQA, a 1.5M-sample scale-layered RS-VQA corpus whose question-answer generation is conditioned on the same physical scalar that drives CS-HLoRA, forming a closed method-data loop. Trained with QLoRA on an 8B backbone, ScaleEarth achieves state-of-the-art results on remote-sensing benchmarks covering diverse Earth-system tasks, including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench. Published: 2026-05-08T10:35:11+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Song Zhang; Yanlong Chen; Yilin Li; Yining Chen; Zili Yi; Xiaowei Zhang; Yawei Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Remote sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs) face a fundamental mismatch with natural-image counterparts: the same geographic object exhibits radically different visual evidence across ground sampling distances (GSDs) spanning multiple orders of magnitude. Yet existing RS-VLMs often discard GSD or inject it as a discrete text token, forcing a single static parameter set to absorb the entire scale spectrum. We introduce ScaleEarth, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework built on Qwen3-VL that treats GSD as a continuous conditioning variable governing the model's computation path. At its core, CS-HLoRA (Continuous Scale-Conditioned Hyper-LoRA) modulates the LoRA low-rank subspace through a GSD-driven gate, enabling the model to dynamically route computation by physical scale. To remove reliance on sensor metadata at deployment, we pair CS-HLoRA with SSE-U, a lightweight heteroscedastic sub-head that predicts GSD and its uncertainty from visual features. To provide matching supervision, we construct GeoScale-VQA, a 1.5M-sample scale-layered RS-VQA corpus whose question-answer generation is conditioned on the same physical scalar that drives CS-HLoRA, forming a closed method-data loop. Trained with QLoRA on an 8B backbone, ScaleEarth achieves state-of-the-art results on remote-sensing benchmarks covering diverse Earth-system tasks, including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench.

item.title: Prompt-Free and Efficient SAM2 Adaptation for Biomedical Semantic Segmentation via Dual Adapters
item.dc:creator: Hinako Mitsuoka
Kazuhiro Hotta
item.description: Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) demonstrated impressive zero-shot capabilities on natural images but faces challenges in biomedical segmentation due to significant domain shifts and prompt dependency. To address these limitations, we propose a prompt-free, parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework designed for multi-class segmentation on variable-sized inputs. We introduce a convolutional Positional Encoding Generator to adapt effectively to arbitrary aspect ratios and present a dual-adapter strategy: High-Performance Adapter utilizing deformable convolutions for precise boundary modeling and Lightweight Adapter employing structural re-parameterization to minimize inference latency. Experiments on ISBI 2012, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms strong adaptation baselines. Specifically, our method improved segmentation accuracy by up to 19.66\% over the vanilla SAM2, while reducing computational costs by approximately 87\% compared to heavyweight medical SAM adaptations, establishing a superior trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Published: 2026-05-07T10:24:01+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hinako Mitsuoka; Kazuhiro Hotta

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) demonstrated impressive zero-shot capabilities on natural images but faces challenges in biomedical segmentation due to significant domain shifts and prompt dependency. To address these limitations, we propose a prompt-free, parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework designed for multi-class segmentation on variable-sized inputs. We introduce a convolutional Positional Encoding Generator to adapt effectively to arbitrary aspect ratios and present a dual-adapter strategy: High-Performance Adapter utilizing deformable convolutions for precise boundary modeling and Lightweight Adapter employing structural re-parameterization to minimize inference latency. Experiments on ISBI 2012, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms strong adaptation baselines. Specifically, our method improved segmentation accuracy by up to 19.66\% over the vanilla SAM2, while reducing computational costs by approximately 87\% compared to heavyweight medical SAM adaptations, establishing a superior trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

item.title: XiYOLO: Energy-Aware Object Detection via Iterative Architecture Search and Scaling
item.dc:creator: Tony Tran
Richie R. Suganda
Bin Hu
item.description: Object detection on heterogeneous edge devices must satisfy strict energy, latency, and memory constraints while still providing reliable perception for downstream autonomy. Existing energy-aware NAS methods often target limited deployment settings, while real energy remains difficult to optimize because it is highly device-dependent and costly to measure. We address these challenges with an energy-adaptive framework that combines an energy-aware XiResOFA search space, a two-stage energy estimator, and iterative search to identify a single energy-efficient base architecture. We then apply compound scaling to transform this base design into the XiYOLO family across deployment budgets, enabling interpretable accuracy-energy tradeoffs under sparse hardware measurements. Experiments on PascalVOC, COCO, and real-device deployment show that XiYOLO achieves a stronger energy-accuracy tradeoff than YOLO baselines. On PascalVOC, the medium XiYOLO model reaches 86.15 mAP50 while reducing energy relative to YOLOv12m by 20.6% on GPU and 35.9% on NPU. On COCO, XiYOLO reduces energy relative to YOLOv12 by up to 53.7% on GPU and 51.6% on NPU at the small scale. The proposed two-stage estimator also improves sample efficiency over a joint predictor under few-shot adaptation with only 2-20 target-device samples. Published: 2026-05-07T20:38:06+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.780 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tony Tran; Richie R. Suganda; Bin Hu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Object detection on heterogeneous edge devices must satisfy strict energy, latency, and memory constraints while still providing reliable perception for downstream autonomy. Existing energy-aware NAS methods often target limited deployment settings, while real energy remains difficult to optimize because it is highly device-dependent and costly to measure. We address these challenges with an energy-adaptive framework that combines an energy-aware XiResOFA search space, a two-stage energy estimator, and iterative search to identify a single energy-efficient base architecture. We then apply compound scaling to transform this base design into the XiYOLO family across deployment budgets, enabling interpretable accuracy-energy tradeoffs under sparse hardware measurements. Experiments on PascalVOC, COCO, and real-device deployment show that XiYOLO achieves a stronger energy-accuracy tradeoff than YOLO baselines. On PascalVOC, the medium XiYOLO model reaches 86.15 mAP50 while reducing energy relative to YOLOv12m by 20.6% on GPU and 35.9% on NPU. On COCO, XiYOLO reduces energy relative to YOLOv12 by up to 53.7% on GPU and 51.6% on NPU at the small scale. The proposed two-stage estimator also improves sample efficiency over a joint predictor under few-shot adaptation with only 2-20 target-device samples.

item.title: Align3D-AD: Cross-Modal Feature Alignment and Dual-Prompt Learning for Zero-shot 3D Anomaly Detection
item.dc:creator: Letian Bai
Xuanming Cao
Juan Du
Chengyu Tao
item.description: Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection aims to identify anomalies without access to training data from target categories. However, existing methods mainly rely on projecting 3D observations into multi-view representations that primarily capture geometric cues rather than realistic visual semantics and process them with vision encoders pretrained on RGB data, leading to a significant domain gap between the encoder and the projected representations. To address this issue, we propose Align3D-AD, a unified two-stage framework that leverages the RGB modality from auxiliary categories as cross-modal guidance for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection. First, we introduce a cross-modal feature alignment paradigm that maps rendering features into the RGB semantic space. Unlike prior works that implicitly rely on pretrained encoders, our method enables direct semantic transfer from RGB observations. A semantic consistency reweighting strategy is further introduced to refine feature alignment by reweighting local regions according to holistic semantic consistency. Second, we propose a modality-aware prompt learning framework with dual-prompt contrastive alignment. By assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features, our method captures complementary semantics across modalities, while the contrastive alignment further enhances prompt representations to improve discriminability. Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, and Real3D-AD demonstrate that Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods under both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, highlighting its generalization capability and robustness. Code and the dataset will be made available once our paper is accepted. Published: 2026-05-07T08:24:44+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Letian Bai; Xuanming Cao; Juan Du; Chengyu Tao

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection aims to identify anomalies without access to training data from target categories. However, existing methods mainly rely on projecting 3D observations into multi-view representations that primarily capture geometric cues rather than realistic visual semantics and process them with vision encoders pretrained on RGB data, leading to a significant domain gap between the encoder and the projected representations. To address this issue, we propose Align3D-AD, a unified two-stage framework that leverages the RGB modality from auxiliary categories as cross-modal guidance for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection. First, we introduce a cross-modal feature alignment paradigm that maps rendering features into the RGB semantic space. Unlike prior works that implicitly rely on pretrained encoders, our method enables direct semantic transfer from RGB observations. A semantic consistency reweighting strategy is further introduced to refine feature alignment by reweighting local regions according to holistic semantic consistency. Second, we propose a modality-aware prompt learning framework with dual-prompt contrastive alignment. By assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features, our method captures complementary semantics across modalities, while the contrastive alignment further enhances prompt representations to improve discriminability. Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, and Real3D-AD demonstrate that Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods under both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, highlighting its generalization capability and robustness. Code and the dataset will be made available once our paper is accepted.

item.title: RA-PTQ: Reparameterization-Aware Post-Training Quantization for accurate vision transformers in low-bit scenarios
item.dc:creator: Rui Ding
Sihuan Zhao
Jiayu Shi
Jing Nie
Lihui Chen
Haijun Liu
Xichuan Zhou
item.description: Due to the increasing model size, the efficient Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) gradually becomes a prominent method for compressing the Vision Transformers (ViTs) compared with the time-consuming Quantization-Aware Training (QAT). The previous works propose the scale reparameterization technique to keep the hardware-friendly property while retaining the model performance. However, we empirically find that the reparameterization causes significant performance degeneration in low-bit quantization scenarios based on different ViT backbones. To solve this problem, we propose an innovative PTQ framework named RA-PTQ to enable the reparameterization-aware quantization for accurate low-bit ViTs. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive logarithmic quantization to adapt to the different data distributions and quantization bit-widths, reducing the quantization errors in post-softmax and post-GELU activations. Secondly, instead of performing the reparameterization of channel-wise quantizers in Post-LayerNorm activations sequentially, we propose to conduct this process with a two-stage reparameterization-aware calibration. Finally, to further alleviate the output deviation induced by the reparameterization, we propose a reparameterization-aware reconstruction strategy that contains two reconstruction optimization processes, improving the model performance significantly in low-bit quantization. Experimental results on the ImageNet and COCO datasets demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms other state-of-the-art PTQ works in image classification, object detection, and instance segmentation tasks. Besides, our method strikes a decent trade-off between model performance and quantization efficiency, which constructs a strong baseline for future work. The code will be available when this paper is accepted. Published: 2026-05-09T15:15:17+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rui Ding; Sihuan Zhao; Jiayu Shi; Jing Nie; Lihui Chen; Haijun Liu; Xichuan Zhou

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116114

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Due to the increasing model size, the efficient Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) gradually becomes a prominent method for compressing the Vision Transformers (ViTs) compared with the time-consuming Quantization-Aware Training (QAT). The previous works propose the scale reparameterization technique to keep the hardware-friendly property while retaining the model performance. However, we empirically find that the reparameterization causes significant performance degeneration in low-bit quantization scenarios based on different ViT backbones. To solve this problem, we propose an innovative PTQ framework named RA-PTQ to enable the reparameterization-aware quantization for accurate low-bit ViTs. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive logarithmic quantization to adapt to the different data distributions and quantization bit-widths, reducing the quantization errors in post-softmax and post-GELU activations. Secondly, instead of performing the reparameterization of channel-wise quantizers in Post-LayerNorm activations sequentially, we propose to conduct this process with a two-stage reparameterization-aware calibration. Finally, to further alleviate the output deviation induced by the reparameterization, we propose a reparameterization-aware reconstruction strategy that contains two reconstruction optimization processes, improving the model performance significantly in low-bit quantization. Experimental results on the ImageNet and COCO datasets demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms other state-of-the-art PTQ works in image classification, object detection, and instance segmentation tasks. Besides, our method strikes a decent trade-off between model performance and quantization efficiency, which constructs a strong baseline for future work. The code will be available when this paper is accepted.

item.title: Steering Visual Generation in Unified Multimodal Models with Understanding Supervision
item.dc:creator: Zeyu Liu
Zanlin Ni
Yang Yue
Cheng Da
Huan Yang
Di Zhang
Kun Gai
Gao Huang
item.description: Unified multimodal models are envisioned to bridge the gap between understanding and generation. Yet, to achieve competitive performance, state-of-the-art models adopt largely decoupled understanding and generation components. This design, while effective for individual tasks, weakens the connection required for mutual enhancement, leaving the potential synergy empirically uncertain. We propose to explicitly restore this synergy by introducing Understanding-Oriented Post-Training (UNO), a lightweight framework that treats understanding not only as a distinct task, but also a direct supervisory signal to steer generative representations. By incorporating objectives that encode semantic abstraction (captioning) and structural details (visual regression), we enable effective gradient flow from understanding to generation. Extensive experiments on image generation and editing demonstrate that understanding can serve as an effective catalyst for generation. Published: 2026-05-07T07:20:04+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zeyu Liu; Zanlin Ni; Yang Yue; Cheng Da; Huan Yang; Di Zhang; Kun Gai; Gao Huang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Unified multimodal models are envisioned to bridge the gap between understanding and generation. Yet, to achieve competitive performance, state-of-the-art models adopt largely decoupled understanding and generation components. This design, while effective for individual tasks, weakens the connection required for mutual enhancement, leaving the potential synergy empirically uncertain. We propose to explicitly restore this synergy by introducing Understanding-Oriented Post-Training (UNO), a lightweight framework that treats understanding not only as a distinct task, but also a direct supervisory signal to steer generative representations. By incorporating objectives that encode semantic abstraction (captioning) and structural details (visual regression), we enable effective gradient flow from understanding to generation. Extensive experiments on image generation and editing demonstrate that understanding can serve as an effective catalyst for generation.

item.title: UniD-Shift: Towards Unified Semantic Segmentation via Interpretable Share-Private Multimodal Decomposition
item.dc:creator: Shuai Zhang
Zhecheng Shi
Zhuxiao Li
Jing Ou
Tengxi Wang
Yuan Liu
Wufan Zhao
item.description: Semantic segmentation of large-scale 3D point clouds is crucial for applications such as autonomous driving and urban digital twins. However, the sparse sampling pattern of LiDAR and the view-dependent geometric distortion in image observations complicate cross-modal alignment and hinder stable fusion. Inspired by the fact that 2D images captured by cameras are representations of the 3D world, we recognize that the features learned from 2D and 3D segmentation share some common semantics, while other aspects remain modality-specific. This insight motivates a unified multimodal framework for joint 2D-3D semantic segmentation. We combine a SAM-based vision encoder with a SPTNet-based geometric encoder to extract complementary semantic and geometric representations. The resulting features from both modalities are explicitly decomposed into shared and private subspaces, where the shared components summarize semantic factors common to both domains, and the private components preserve properties that are unique to each modality. A lightweight attention-based fusion module aggregates the shared features into a consistent cross-modal representation, and a regularized training objective ensures both semantic alignment and subspace independence. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in segmentation accuracy over representative multimodal baselines, accompanied by competitive computational efficiency. Cross-domain evaluation on nuScenes USA-Singapore shows stable performance under distribution shifts, demonstrating strong generalization. The implementation code is publicly available at: https://github.com/shuaizhang69/UniD-Shift. Published: 2026-05-08T07:09:08+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuai Zhang; Zhecheng Shi; Zhuxiao Li; Jing Ou; Tengxi Wang; Yuan Liu; Wufan Zhao

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Semantic segmentation of large-scale 3D point clouds is crucial for applications such as autonomous driving and urban digital twins. However, the sparse sampling pattern of LiDAR and the view-dependent geometric distortion in image observations complicate cross-modal alignment and hinder stable fusion. Inspired by the fact that 2D images captured by cameras are representations of the 3D world, we recognize that the features learned from 2D and 3D segmentation share some common semantics, while other aspects remain modality-specific. This insight motivates a unified multimodal framework for joint 2D-3D semantic segmentation. We combine a SAM-based vision encoder with a SPTNet-based geometric encoder to extract complementary semantic and geometric representations. The resulting features from both modalities are explicitly decomposed into shared and private subspaces, where the shared components summarize semantic factors common to both domains, and the private components preserve properties that are unique to each modality. A lightweight attention-based fusion module aggregates the shared features into a consistent cross-modal representation, and a regularized training objective ensures both semantic alignment and subspace independence. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in segmentation accuracy over representative multimodal baselines, accompanied by competitive computational efficiency. Cross-domain evaluation on nuScenes USA-Singapore shows stable performance under distribution shifts, demonstrating strong generalization. The implementation code is publicly available at: https://github.com/shuaizhang69/UniD-Shift.

item.title: YOLO-FR: An Enhanced Object Detector for Small Object Detection in Complex Scenes
item.dc:creator: Rui Fan
Renhao Jiao
Weigui Nan
Jin Dang
Zhixue Wang
Xuefeng Ge
Baiying Dong
Xiaoli Luo
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment
item.description: As a key technology, small object detection has shown broad application prospects in the fields of UAV aerial images, remote sensing images, medical diagnosis, intelligent transportation and industrial quality inspection. However, due to the small size of the small object itself, it is susceptible to factors such as low resolution, limited semantic information, and complex background interference, resulting in its detection performance still facing severe challenges. To this end, we propose an improved object detection model YOLO-FR, which aims to improve the detection accuracy and robustness of small objects in complex scenes. Firstly, the C3k2_WT module is designed to expand the receptive field layer by layer and capture the context information by using the cascaded frequency decomposition characteristics of wavelet transform. At the same time, a C3k2_ECA module integrated with Efficient Channel Attention is designed to effectively reduce the number of parameters and enhance the selective response of channel features. Secondly, a small object detection head is introduced to improve the model’s support for small object detection. Additionally, the Quad Fusion Downsample downsampling module is proposed to reduce information loss and improve downsampling efficiency. Finally, we compare a variety of emerging Mamba modules to determine the most suitable module for the task of this article, and optimize it on this basis. Our model improves mAP@50:95 by 6.6% and 2.2% on the public benchmark datasets VisDrone2019 and AI-TOD, respectively. At the same time, the parameters of the model are reduced by 36.5%. We also evaluate the segmentation performance of the model on the UAV instance segmentation dataset UAVid, which demonstrates the advancement and applicability of the proposed model in the face of different tasks. The source code will be available at https://github.com/unscfr/YOLO-FR . Published: 2026-05-09T15:41:12+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rui Fan; Renhao Jiao; Weigui Nan; Jin Dang; Zhixue Wang; Xuefeng Ge; Baiying Dong; Xiaoli Luo

Journal: Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rsase.2026.102059

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

As a key technology, small object detection has shown broad application prospects in the fields of UAV aerial images, remote sensing images, medical diagnosis, intelligent transportation and industrial quality inspection. However, due to the small size of the small object itself, it is susceptible to factors such as low resolution, limited semantic information, and complex background interference, resulting in its detection performance still facing severe challenges. To this end, we propose an improved object detection model YOLO-FR, which aims to improve the detection accuracy and robustness of small objects in complex scenes. Firstly, the C3k2_WT module is designed to expand the receptive field layer by layer and capture the context information by using the cascaded frequency decomposition characteristics of wavelet transform. At the same time, a C3k2_ECA module integrated with Efficient Channel Attention is designed to effectively reduce the number of parameters and enhance the selective response of channel features. Secondly, a small object detection head is introduced to improve the model’s support for small object detection. Additionally, the Quad Fusion Downsample downsampling module is proposed to reduce information loss and improve downsampling efficiency. Finally, we compare a variety of emerging Mamba modules to determine the most suitable module for the task of this article, and optimize it on this basis. Our model improves mAP@50:95 by 6.6% and 2.2% on the public benchmark datasets VisDrone2019 and AI-TOD, respectively. At the same time, the parameters of the model are reduced by 36.5%. We also evaluate the segmentation performance of the model on the UAV instance segmentation dataset UAVid, which demonstrates the advancement and applicability of the proposed model in the face of different tasks. The source code will be available at https://github.com/unscfr/YOLO-FR .

item.title: Generalizable UAV-based image registration framework for enhancing visual inspection of civil infrastructure
item.dc:creator: Anas Alsharo
Max Midwinter
Chul Min Yeum
Jongseong Choi
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Managing and retrieving information from large-scale images dataset collected using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), like drones, remains a significant challenge in the field of visual inspection. To overcome this challenge, this study proposes a generalizable and accurate image registration algorithm that enables inspectors to register any collected new images of the structure to an existing base Structure from Motion (SfM) model of the structure regardless of the used SfM platform and without the need for a database of image descriptors or 3D point cloud for the registration. Registered new images are used to detect Regions of Interest (ROIs) from old images into new images of the structure. The performance of the proposed algorithm was tested on publicly available high-resolution multi-view stereo benchmark and on publicly available concrete bridge photogrammetry data. The results show that the proposed approach exhibits competent performance of estimating poses including angles and locations compared to existing algorithms. Additionally, the algorithm was tested experimentally using data collected by a commercial drone for telecommunication cell tower and the ROI retrieval and localization within new images was demonstrated. The experiment employed images collected at different viewpoints, lighting conditions, background visibility, and camera type (e.g., different sensor size or focal length). Published: 2026-05-09T10:28:55+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Anas Alsharo; Max Midwinter; Chul Min Yeum; Jongseong Choi

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.053

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Managing and retrieving information from large-scale images dataset collected using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), like drones, remains a significant challenge in the field of visual inspection. To overcome this challenge, this study proposes a generalizable and accurate image registration algorithm that enables inspectors to register any collected new images of the structure to an existing base Structure from Motion (SfM) model of the structure regardless of the used SfM platform and without the need for a database of image descriptors or 3D point cloud for the registration. Registered new images are used to detect Regions of Interest (ROIs) from old images into new images of the structure. The performance of the proposed algorithm was tested on publicly available high-resolution multi-view stereo benchmark and on publicly available concrete bridge photogrammetry data. The results show that the proposed approach exhibits competent performance of estimating poses including angles and locations compared to existing algorithms. Additionally, the algorithm was tested experimentally using data collected by a commercial drone for telecommunication cell tower and the ROI retrieval and localization within new images was demonstrated. The experiment employed images collected at different viewpoints, lighting conditions, background visibility, and camera type (e.g., different sensor size or focal length).

item.title: Evaluating and Regulating Agentic AI: A Study of Benchmarks, Metrics, and Regulation
item.dc:creator: Azib Farooq
Shaina Raza
Nazmul Karim
Hasan Iqbal
Athanasios V. Vasilakos
Christos Emmanouilidis
item.description: Agentic AI represents a new generation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems capable of perceiving, reasoning, planning, and acting toward achieving goals with a degree of autonomy. Unlike traditional AI models that merely generate outputs, these systems maintain memory, interact with their environment, and adapt over time. However, evaluating such interactive and evolving behavior remains a significant challenge. While several recent surveys have examined agentic AI architectures, components, and applications, few have systematically reviewed their evaluation, particularly with respect to performance, reliability, and governance across an evolving agentic AI ecosystem. This survey paper addresses that gap by reviewing recent progress in the evaluation and assessment of agentic AI, focusing on three core dimensions: benchmarks, metrics, and governance. We analyze how current evaluation frameworks capture reasoning, planning, collaboration, and ethical alignment across single- and multi-agent systems. Ultimately, this study aims to establish a unified foundation for building trustworthy, auditable, and human-aligned AI agents. Published: 2026-05-09T15:38:01+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Azib Farooq; Shaina Raza; Nazmul Karim; Hasan Iqbal; Athanasios V. Vasilakos; Christos Emmanouilidis

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104444

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Agentic AI represents a new generation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems capable of perceiving, reasoning, planning, and acting toward achieving goals with a degree of autonomy. Unlike traditional AI models that merely generate outputs, these systems maintain memory, interact with their environment, and adapt over time. However, evaluating such interactive and evolving behavior remains a significant challenge. While several recent surveys have examined agentic AI architectures, components, and applications, few have systematically reviewed their evaluation, particularly with respect to performance, reliability, and governance across an evolving agentic AI ecosystem. This survey paper addresses that gap by reviewing recent progress in the evaluation and assessment of agentic AI, focusing on three core dimensions: benchmarks, metrics, and governance. We analyze how current evaluation frameworks capture reasoning, planning, collaboration, and ethical alignment across single- and multi-agent systems. Ultimately, this study aims to establish a unified foundation for building trustworthy, auditable, and human-aligned AI agents.

item.title: Attention Transfer Is Not Universally Effective for Vision Transformers
item.dc:creator: Huaiyuan Qin
Muli Yang
Gabriel James Goenawan
Peng Hu
Chen Gong
Xi Peng
Hongyuan Zhu
item.description: A recent work shows that Attention Transfer, which transfers only the attention patterns from a pre-trained teacher Vision Transformer (ViT) to a randomly initialized standard student ViT, is sufficient to recover the full benefit of the teacher's pre-trained weights. We revisit this finding on a comprehensive benchmark of 20 teachers from 11 well-known ViT families and reveal that Attention Transfer is not universally effective. While 7 families transfer successfully, 4 consistently fail, falling up to 5.1\% below the from-scratch no-transfer baseline. Further results demonstrate that this failure is family-consistent across model sizes, and persists under extended training durations, different transfer datasets, and out-of-distribution evaluations. Controlled analyses then consistently localize the problem to the attention-routing channel, indicating that the key issue is not whether the student can match the teacher's attention patterns, but whether the matched patterns remain functional for the student. Crucially, we identify architectural mismatch between the pre-trained teacher and the standard student as the primary mechanism. By adding only the teacher's native architectural components to the student in a randomly initialized state, we completely reverse the failure for all 4 families. Notably, these components alone do not improve from-scratch training, confirming that they specifically unlock the usability of the teacher's attention. We further systematically show that this failure is not explained by the inadequate choice of transfer loss or by differences in pre-training recipes. Our findings refine the prevailing understanding of attention in ViT representations: attention is sufficient \textit{only} when the student architecture matches the teacher. Published: 2026-05-08T03:39:38+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.772 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huaiyuan Qin; Muli Yang; Gabriel James Goenawan; Peng Hu; Chen Gong; Xi Peng; Hongyuan Zhu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

A recent work shows that Attention Transfer, which transfers only the attention patterns from a pre-trained teacher Vision Transformer (ViT) to a randomly initialized standard student ViT, is sufficient to recover the full benefit of the teacher's pre-trained weights. We revisit this finding on a comprehensive benchmark of 20 teachers from 11 well-known ViT families and reveal that Attention Transfer is not universally effective. While 7 families transfer successfully, 4 consistently fail, falling up to 5.1\% below the from-scratch no-transfer baseline. Further results demonstrate that this failure is family-consistent across model sizes, and persists under extended training durations, different transfer datasets, and out-of-distribution evaluations. Controlled analyses then consistently localize the problem to the attention-routing channel, indicating that the key issue is not whether the student can match the teacher's attention patterns, but whether the matched patterns remain functional for the student. Crucially, we identify architectural mismatch between the pre-trained teacher and the standard student as the primary mechanism. By adding only the teacher's native architectural components to the student in a randomly initialized state, we completely reverse the failure for all 4 families. Notably, these components alone do not improve from-scratch training, confirming that they specifically unlock the usability of the teacher's attention. We further systematically show that this failure is not explained by the inadequate choice of transfer loss or by differences in pre-training recipes. Our findings refine the prevailing understanding of attention in ViT representations: attention is sufficient \textit{only} when the student architecture matches the teacher.

item.title: Large Vision-Language Models Get Lost in Attention
item.dc:creator: Gongli Xi
Ye Tian
Mengyu Yang
Huahui Yi
Liang Lin
Xiaoshuai Hao
Kun Wang
Wendong Wang
item.description: Despite the rapid evolution of training paradigms, the decoder backbone of large vision--language models (LVLMs) remains fundamentally rooted in the residual-connection Transformer architecture. Therefore, deciphering the distinct roles of internal modules is critical for understanding model mechanics and guiding architectural optimization. While prior statistical approaches have provided valuable attribution-based insights, they often lack a unified theoretical basis. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates. Applying this unified framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator focused on reconfiguration, whereas FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Strikingly, further experiments demonstrate that replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) yields comparable or even superior performance across a majority of datasets relative to vanilla models. These results expose severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms, suggesting that state-of-the-art LVLMs effectively ``get lost in attention'' rather than efficiently leveraging visual context. Published: 2026-05-07T04:45:52+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.770 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gongli Xi; Ye Tian; Mengyu Yang; Huahui Yi; Liang Lin; Xiaoshuai Hao; Kun Wang; Wendong Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.770 (must_read)

Despite the rapid evolution of training paradigms, the decoder backbone of large vision--language models (LVLMs) remains fundamentally rooted in the residual-connection Transformer architecture. Therefore, deciphering the distinct roles of internal modules is critical for understanding model mechanics and guiding architectural optimization. While prior statistical approaches have provided valuable attribution-based insights, they often lack a unified theoretical basis. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates. Applying this unified framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator focused on reconfiguration, whereas FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Strikingly, further experiments demonstrate that replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) yields comparable or even superior performance across a majority of datasets relative to vanilla models. These results expose severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms, suggesting that state-of-the-art LVLMs effectively ``get lost in attention'' rather than efficiently leveraging visual context.

item.title: Sat3R: Satellite DSM Reconstruction via RPC-Aware Depth Fine-tuning
item.dc:creator: Qiaoyi Yang
Chaoyi Zhou
Xi Liu
Run Wang
Minghui Xu
Mert D. Pesé
Feng Luo
Yuhao Xu
Zhi-Qi Cheng
Qiushi Chen
Hairong Qi
Siyu Huang
item.description: Accurate Digital Surface Model (DSM) reconstruction from satellite imagery is critical for applications such as disaster response, urban planning, and large-scale geographic mapping. Existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off: optimization-based methods achieve strong accuracy but require hours of per-scene computation, while generalizable geometry foundation models offer near-instant inference but fail to generalize to satellite imagery due to the domain gap introduced by the Rational Polynomial Camera (RPC) model and mismatched depth scale distributions. We present Sat3R, a feed-forward framework that bridges this gap via RPC-aware metric depth fine-tuning of Depth Anything V2 using the Scale-Invariant Logarithmic (SiLog) loss. By constructing physically consistent pseudo depth supervision from RPC geometry, Sat3R adapts a monocular depth foundation model to the satellite domain without per-scene optimization. Experiments on the DFC2019 benchmark demonstrate that Sat3R reduces MAE by 38% over zero-shot feed-forward baselines and achieves competitive accuracy against optimization-based methods, while delivering over 300x speedup. Sat3R demonstrates that feed-forward models, when properly adapted to the satellite domain, can match optimization-based accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost, paving the way for practical large-scale satellite DSM reconstruction. Published: 2026-05-08T05:29:55+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.769 (must_read)
